content.text: Regulatory and Customer Notification for Major Incidents

When a major incident affects user data, violates an enterprise SLA, or exceeds a customer-impact threshold, the obligations extend beyond a status page update. Regulatory frameworks impose mandatory notification timelines. Enterprise contracts specify SLA credit and notification procedures. Industry norms create reputational expectations around public post-incident reporting. This sub-subtopic maps those obligations to the incident classification system and provides a practical implementation framework for meeting each one.

Notification obligations are not discretionary. Missing a regulatory notification deadline can result in fines. Failing to notify enterprise customers of an SLA breach breaches the contract. The decision of whether to notify and by when must be made by Legal and the executive team, not by the engineering team acting alone — but engineering must supply the facts (affected users, data categories, timeline) that enable those decisions.

attrs.alt: Notification decision framework: Incident Resolved → Privacy/data exposure? Yes → Regulatory Track (GDPR 72h etc); No → SLA Breach Check. All paths lead to three notification types: Regulatory (DPA/authority), Public Post-Incident (blog/status page), Enterprise Direct (TAM/email to affected accounts).

content.text: Regulatory Notification Obligations

Regulatory notification requirements apply when an incident involves the exposure, loss, or unauthorised access of personal data. The two most commonly applicable frameworks for an AI company serving global users are GDPR (EU/EEA) and US state privacy laws (CCPA and its amendments, and state breach notification statutes).
Framework
Trigger
Notification Target
Deadline
Required Content
GDPR (EU/EEA)
Personal data breach likely to result in risk to individuals
Lead supervisory authority (DPA)
72 hours from discovery
Categories of data, approximate number of records, likely consequences, measures taken
GDPR — high risk
Breach likely to result in high risk to individuals
Affected data subjects (users)
Without undue delay
Nature of breach, DPO contact, likely consequences, remediation steps
US state breach laws (e.g. CA, NY)
Unauthorised access to defined categories of personal information
Affected residents (individuals) + AG in some states
Varies by state: CA "expedient time", NY 30 days
Description of breach, data categories, contact for questions

The 72-hour GDPR clock starts at discovery, not at resolution. If an incident is discovered on day 1 and resolved on day 3, the regulatory notification deadline may already be at risk by the time the postmortem is complete. Legal must be looped in at incident declaration for any incident that might involve personal data, not after resolution.

Legal, not engineering, determines whether a given incident constitutes a reportable breach under each applicable framework. Engineering supplies the technical facts: what data was accessible, for how long, to whom, and what logging confirms or refutes that it was accessed. That factual input must be available within hours, not days.
Enterprise Customer Notification

Enterprise customers have contractual SLA terms that specify notification obligations when service levels are not met. These terms vary by contract, but common provisions include: notification within 24–48 hours of any P0/P1 incident affecting the customer, a written incident report within 5 business days, and a credit calculation mechanism for downtime exceeding the SLA threshold.

The process for enterprise notification involves four parties: the Account Manager (who knows which customers were affected and manages the relationship), Legal (who reviews the notification against contract terms), Comms (who approves the language), and Engineering (who provides the technical facts). The notification must not make admissions of fault beyond what was agreed in the SLA, but must be accurate and complete.

The notification email to enterprise customers follows a specific structure: incident summary (dates, duration, affected services), customer-specific impact (which of their API calls failed), what was done to mitigate, and — if the SLA was breached — confirmation of credit and how it will be applied. The Account Manager sends the email; engineering provides the data.
Public Post-Incident Report

A public post-incident report is published to the status page or company blog for incidents above a user-impact threshold. It differs from the technical postmortem in three important ways: it is written for customers rather than engineers, it does not include internal technical details that could create security exposure, and it is reviewed by Legal before publication.
Trigger:
Any P0 incident, or any P1 with more than 1 million users affected, or any incident involving personal data. The decision is made at the exec review.
Timeline:
Published within 5 business days of incident resolution, after the exec review and Legal sign-off.
Structure:

Summary (what happened, when, impact), What We Did (timeline in plain language), Root Cause (one-sentence customer-safe explanation), What We Are Doing (2–3 concrete improvement commitments). No internal system names, no internal blame language, no details that would help an attacker exploit the same weakness.
Approval chain:
Engineering drafts → Comms edits for tone → Legal reviews for admissions → VP Engineering approves → published.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

PROHIBITED_PHRASES = [
    "human error",
    "engineer mistake",
    "we failed to",
    "negligence",
    "should have caught",
    "our fault",
]

REQUIRED_SECTIONS = [
    "summary",
    "what_happened",
    "root_cause",
    "what_we_are_doing",
]

@dataclass
class PublicIncidentReport:
    incident_id: str
    publish_date: date
    summary: str
    what_happened: str        # plain-language timeline, no internal names
    root_cause: str           # one-sentence customer-safe cause
    what_we_are_doing: str    # concrete commitments, no vague reassurances
    legal_approved_at: date | None = None
    comms_approved_at: date | None = None
    vp_eng_approved_at: date | None = None

    def lint(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        full_text = " ".join([
            self.summary, self.what_happened, self.root_cause, self.what_we_are_doing
        ]).lower()
        for phrase in PROHIBITED_PHRASES:
            if phrase in full_text:
                errors.append(f"Prohibited phrase found: '{phrase}'")
        if not self.legal_approved_at:
            errors.append("Legal approval required before publication")
        if not self.comms_approved_at:
            errors.append("Comms approval required before publication")
        if not self.vp_eng_approved_at:
            errors.append("VP Engineering approval required before publication")
        return errors

    def can_publish(self) -> bool:
        return len(self.lint()) == 0
Notification Tracking and Audit Trail

All notification activities must be logged with timestamps. This serves two purposes: demonstrating regulatory compliance ("we notified the DPA at T+68h") and providing an audit trail for contract disputes with enterprise customers. The notification log is immutable — entries are appended, never edited.
AI-Specific Notification Considerations
AI services present notification edge cases that do not arise in classical software incidents. Each requires specific treatment.
Conversation data exposure:

If user conversation content was accessible to other users or to unauthorised parties, this is a personal data breach under GDPR regardless of whether the content is formally "sensitive". The GDPR clock starts immediately. Conservatively estimate the number of conversations that may have been exposed and notify Legal within the first hour.
Model output quality degradation:

A degradation in model output quality is not typically a regulatory notification event, but it may be a contractual one. Enterprise customers using the API for regulated use cases (medical documentation, legal drafting) may have addendums requiring notification of model changes or quality events. These must be checked against individual contract terms, not treated uniformly.
Prompt injection or adversarial exploit:

If an incident was caused by a prompt injection attack that caused the model to output or relay user data, this is both a security incident and potentially a data breach. The response must coordinate the security incident process (preserve evidence, patch the vector) with the notification process (assess what data was affected, notify as required).
API key exposure:

If API keys belonging to customers were exposed or logged in error, immediate rotation must be coordinated with the customer notification — notifying a customer that their key was exposed before rotating it gives an attacker a window. The sequence must be: rotate first, then notify.
Engineering Fact Package

Legal and the executive team make notification decisions, but they can only do so with accurate technical facts. The engineering team must produce a fact package within a defined window after the incident is resolved. The fact package is a structured document, not a narrative — Legal needs specific fields to assess obligations under each regulatory framework.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum

class ExposureConfidence(str, Enum):
    CONFIRMED   = "confirmed"    # logs definitively show access
    PROBABLE    = "probable"     # logs suggest access but are incomplete
    POSSIBLE    = "possible"     # vulnerability existed; access cannot be ruled out
    UNLIKELY    = "unlikely"     # vulnerability existed; no evidence of access

@dataclass
class IncidentFactPackage:
    incident_id: str
    severity: str
    discovery_time: datetime
    resolution_time: datetime

    # Data exposure fields (required for regulatory assessment)
    personal_data_involved: bool
    data_categories: list[str]     # e.g. ["email", "conversation_content", "api_key"]
    estimated_records_affected: int
    eu_residents_affected: bool
    us_states_affected: list[str]  # e.g. ["CA", "NY"]
    exposure_confidence: ExposureConfidence
    data_accessible_to: str        # e.g. "other authenticated users", "unauthenticated public", "internal systems only"

    # Service impact fields (required for enterprise SLA assessment)
    api_errors_total: int
    affected_enterprise_account_ids: list[str]
    affected_request_count_per_account: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
    downtime_minutes: float = 0.0

    def ready_for_legal(self) -> list[str]:
        missing = []
        if self.personal_data_involved and not self.data_categories:
            missing.append("data_categories must be populated for personal data incidents")
        if self.personal_data_involved and self.estimated_records_affected == 0:
            missing.append("estimated_records_affected must be > 0 for personal data incidents")
        if self.exposure_confidence == ExposureConfidence.CONFIRMED and not self.data_accessible_to:
            missing.append("data_accessible_to must be specified for confirmed exposures")
        return missing

The fact package must be delivered to Legal within 4 hours of incident resolution for P0 incidents, and within 24 hours for P1 incidents. For incidents where the GDPR 72-hour clock is already running at resolution (discovery happened during the incident), the fact package must be delivered immediately.
Incident Type
Fact Package Due
Owner
P0 with possible data exposure
Within 4 hours of resolution
IC + Engineering lead
P0 without data exposure
Within 12 hours of resolution
IC
P1 with possible data exposure
Within 12 hours of resolution
IC + Engineering lead
P1 without data exposure
Within 24 hours of resolution
EM
Notification Anti-Patterns
Notifying before rotating exposed credentials:
If API keys or tokens were exposed, rotating them after notification gives an attacker the window between disclosure and rotation. Always rotate first, notify second, with as small a gap as possible.
"No evidence of access" as a substitute for notification:

Under GDPR, "no evidence of access" does not exempt from notification if the data was accessible and the risk cannot be ruled out. Legal determines whether notification is required; engineering provides the technical evidence, not the legal conclusion.
Using the postmortem as the customer notification:

The postmortem contains internal system names, blame-free root cause analysis, and details about vulnerability windows. Sending it to customers or regulators is both a confidentiality risk and a poor communication choice. Author the notification separately.
Over-promising in the public report:

Commitments in the public post-incident report ("we will implement X within 30 days") become public expectations. Engineering must vet every commitment in the public report before publication — if the action item is not in the backlog with an owner and a realistic timeline, it must not appear in the public report.
Delaying notification to wait for more certainty:

Regulatory deadlines do not pause while the investigation is ongoing. If the 72-hour clock is running and the investigation is incomplete, submit a partial notification to the DPA with what is known and supplement it when the picture is clearer. Most supervisory authorities explicitly allow phased notifications under GDPR Art. 33(4).
Summary

Major incident notification obligations fall into three categories: regulatory (GDPR 72-hour DPA notification and individual notification for high-risk breaches; US state breach laws), enterprise contractual (SLA breach notification within 48 hours, credit calculation, written incident report within 5 business days), and public (post-incident report for P0 incidents or high user impact, within 5 business days, Legal-approved). All three require a factual input package from engineering: affected users, data categories, incident timeline, and what data was accessible to whom. Legal and the executive team make the notification decisions; engineering supplies the facts. All notifications are logged with timestamps in an immutable audit trail. For AI services, conversation data exposure, prompt injection events, and API key leaks require specific handling that differs from classical software incident notification.
